name: Work on Multiple Branches Simultaneously with Worktrees
description: Use Git worktrees to check out multiple branches in separate directories, enabling parallel work without stashing or context switching.
prerequisites: A repository with at least two branches

steps.title: Create a worktree for an existing branch
steps.description: Creates a new directory at ../project-hotfix with the hotfix/urgent-fix branch checked out. Your main working tree is unaffected.
steps.tip: The directory must not already exist. Git will create it for you.

steps.title: Create a worktree with a new branch
steps.description: Creates a new branch feature/new-api based on main and checks it out in ../project-api. Combines branch creation and worktree setup.

steps.title: List all active worktrees
steps.description: Shows all worktrees, their paths, the checked-out branch, and the HEAD commit for each.

steps.title: Work in the new worktree
steps.command:
cd ../project-hotfix
git status
# Make changes, commit, push as normal
steps.description: Navigate to the worktree directory and work as you normally would. Each worktree is a fully independent checkout sharing the same .git objects.
steps.tip: You can open each worktree in a separate editor window or terminal tab for true parallel development.

steps.title: Remove a worktree when finished
steps.description: Cleans up the worktree directory and unregisters it. The branch itself is not deleted.
steps.warning: If the worktree has uncommitted changes, Git will refuse to remove it. Use --force to override, but you will lose uncommitted work.

steps.title: Prune stale worktree references
steps.description: Removes worktree metadata for directories that were manually deleted instead of using 'git worktree remove'.

fullScript:
#!/usr/bin/env bash
# git-worktree-setup.sh — Create and manage worktrees
set -euo pipefail

ACTION="${1:-list}"
BRANCH="${2:-}"
DIR="${3:-}"

case "$ACTION" in
  create)
    if [ -z "$BRANCH" ]; then
      echo "Usage: $0 create <branch> [directory]"
      exit 1
    fi
    DIR="${DIR:-../$BRANCH}"
    echo "Creating worktree for branch '$BRANCH' at '$DIR'..."
    git worktree add "$DIR" "$BRANCH"
    echo "Done. cd into $DIR to start working."
    ;;
  new)
    if [ -z "$BRANCH" ]; then
      echo "Usage: $0 new <new-branch> [directory] (creates from current HEAD)"
      exit 1
    fi
    DIR="${DIR:-../$BRANCH}"
    echo "Creating new branch '$BRANCH' in worktree '$DIR'..."
    git worktree add -b "$BRANCH" "$DIR"
    echo "Done. cd into $DIR to start working."
    ;;
  remove)
    if [ -z "$BRANCH" ]; then
      echo "Usage: $0 remove <directory>"
      exit 1
    fi
    echo "Removing worktree at '$BRANCH'..."
    git worktree remove "$BRANCH"
    echo "Done."
    ;;
  list|*)
    echo "=== Active worktrees ==="
    git worktree list
    ;;
esac

faq.question: Can two worktrees check out the same branch?
faq.answer: No. Each branch can only be checked out in one worktree at a time. Attempting to check out an already checked-out branch will result in an error.

faq.question: Do worktrees share the same stash and config?
faq.answer: Yes. All worktrees share the same .git directory, which means they share stashes, config, remotes, and object storage. However, each worktree has its own index and working tree.

faq.question: How do worktrees differ from git clone?
faq.answer: A worktree shares the same repository data (objects, refs) as the main checkout, using almost no extra disk space. A clone creates a complete copy of the repository. Worktrees are faster to create and stay in sync automatically.
